{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed change from 7-year to 90-day retention of delivery records on a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets, serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. Framed as infrastructure optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is categorically incorrect and dangerous.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Governance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete transactional record of a delivery including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated commercial documentation." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (PoD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location — a legally significant document in dispute resolution and compliance." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The rules governing how long business records are kept, driven by legal obligations, contractual commitments, and operational need — not by storage cost." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory, tax, customs, and audit obligations — a contractual dependency on data availability." },
      { "term": "Statute of Limitations", "definition": "The legal window during which claims (contractual, tortious, consumer protection) can be brought — delivery records are primary evidence in such claims." },
      { "term": "Commercial Records", "definition": "Transactional documents that EU/UK tax authorities and customs agencies may require during audits, typically with multi-year retention mandates." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that constitute commercial/tax documentation must be retained for the minimum period required by applicable law in each jurisdiction of operation (typically 6-10 years in EU/UK).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Legal mandate. UK Companies Act requires 6 years. German HGB requires 10 years. French Code de Commerce requires 10 years. HMRC can audit VAT records up to 6 years back. Violation exposes the company to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and inability to defend against claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record subject to legal retention requirements is deleted before the applicable statutory retention period expires."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be available for the full duration of the contractual statute of limitations to defend against customer claims and disputes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under English law the limitation period for breach of contract is 6 years (Limitation Act 1980). Under most EU civil codes it is 3-10 years. Deleting evidence within this window makes the company unable to defend claims and creates adverse inference risk in litigation.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while a valid legal claim could still be brought against that delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customers who contractually depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must retain access to that data for the duration specified in their service agreements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Contractual obligation. If B2B customers use our delivery data for their customs declarations, tax filings, or audit trails, deleting that data is a material breach of contract and exposes them to regulatory failure.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while any B2B customer has a contractual right to access it for compliance purposes."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "GDPR data minimization does not override legal retention obligations — data required for legal compliance, contractual performance, or legitimate interest defense is lawfully retained.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 17(3)(b) and (e) explicitly exempt data required for legal compliance and legal claims from the right to erasure. Premature deletion to 'comply with GDPR' when legal retention mandates exist is itself a compliance violation of the other regulation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Retention policy documentation cites the specific legal basis for each retention period and does not delete data that has a valid lawful basis for retention."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Any change to data retention periods must be reviewed by Legal, Compliance, and DPO before implementation — this is not an infrastructure decision.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Process rule, not a state invariant. But violating it creates catastrophic downstream risk. A VP could change the review process, but cannot skip legal review entirely.",
      "testable_assertion": "Retention policy change has documented sign-off from Legal, Compliance, and DPO before any technical implementation begins."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2C customer files a lost-delivery claim 4 months after delivery", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, the delivery record would already be deleted. The company cannot prove delivery occurred, defaults to refund/liability. At scale this creates a fraud vector: customers wait 91 days then claim non-delivery with impunity." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or EU tax authority initiates a VAT audit covering the prior 4 years", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, all delivery records older than 90 days are gone. The company cannot substantiate VAT treatment of deliveries. This results in estimated assessments, penalties, and potential criminal liability for destruction of records." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer is audited by their national customs authority and requests delivery documentation from 18 months ago", "expected_behavior": "Data is gone. The B2B customer fails their audit. They have a breach-of-contract claim against us and will churn. Reputational damage in the B2B segment." },
    { "scenario": "A product liability claim is filed 2 years after delivery, requiring chain-of-custody evidence", "expected_behavior": "Delivery records are gone. The company cannot demonstrate proper handling, creating adverse inference in litigation and increased liability exposure." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer exercises GDPR right to access (DSAR) for a delivery made 60 days ago", "expected_behavior": "Under 90-day policy this works. But if the consumer asks at 91 days and data is deleted, and deletion was NOT legally justified, this could be a GDPR violation for failing to retain data needed to fulfill a foreseeable DSAR." },
    { "scenario": "Insurance claim requires proof of delivery for an item damaged in transit 6 months ago", "expected_behavior": "Record deleted. Insurance claim cannot be substantiated. Company or customer absorbs the loss." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. (1) Regulatory fines: HMRC penalties for missing VAT records range from £400 to 100% of tax due; EU member state penalties are comparable or worse. (2) Litigation exposure: inability to defend delivery disputes, estimated at significant cost depending on volume. (3) B2B customer churn: logistics companies will exit immediately if compliance data disappears. (4) Insurance claim failures: unrecoverable losses. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or lost B2B contract.",
    "operational": "HIGH. (1) Dispute resolution becomes impossible for any delivery older than 90 days — customer service cannot look up records. (2) Analytics, trend analysis, and operational improvement based on historical delivery data are destroyed. (3) Fraud detection models lose training data. (4) Audit readiness drops to zero for anything beyond current quarter.",
    "user": "HIGH. (1) B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries, request proof of delivery, or file delayed claims. (2) B2B customers lose compliance data they contractually depend on — this is a service-breaking change for them. (3) Trust erosion: customers expect a logistics platform to maintain delivery records.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change creates simultaneous legal risk across multiple jurisdictions (UK, EU member states), regulatory risk (tax, customs, data protection), contractual risk (B2B SLA breach), and litigation risk (inability to defend claims). The risk profile is not 'infrastructure optimization' — it is a compliance and legal exposure event.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B logistics customers", "impact": "Loss of compliance data they depend on for tax, customs, and audit — potential breach of contract, customer churn, and reputational damage in enterprise segment." },
      { "group": "B2C consumers", "impact": "Loss of ability to reference deliveries, file claims, or obtain proof of delivery beyond 90 days. Increased fraud opportunity against the platform." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax team", "impact": "Cannot substantiate VAT treatment, customs declarations, or revenue recognition for deliveries older than 90 days. Audit failure risk." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance", "impact": "Cannot defend litigation, respond to regulatory inquiries, or demonstrate compliance with record-keeping mandates." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Cannot resolve disputes or investigate issues for deliveries older than 90 days — degraded service quality." },
      { "group": "Data / Analytics teams", "impact": "Loss of historical data for demand forecasting, route optimization, SLA analysis, and fraud detection." },
      { "group": "Insurance partners", "impact": "Claims for damaged/lost shipments become unsubstantiable beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Executive leadership", "impact": "Personal liability risk in jurisdictions where directors are responsible for maintaining adequate business records." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 92,
    "reasoning": "The legal retention requirements for commercial records in EU and UK jurisdictions are well-established and unambiguous. The 7-year retention period aligns with standard statutory requirements across most EU member states and UK law. The proposed 90-day period is not a gray area — it is categorically below every applicable legal minimum. High confidence that this change as proposed would create material legal and regulatory violations.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK and EU statutory retention periods for commercial records exceed 90 days in every jurisdiction",
      "B2B customers have contractual dependency on delivery data availability",
      "Statute of limitations for contract claims exceeds 90 days in all EU/UK jurisdictions",
      "GDPR does not require deletion of data with valid legal retention basis",
      "This is not an infrastructure-only change — it has first-order legal and business consequences"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods vary by EU member state and specific record type — would need legal review per jurisdiction",
      "Specific B2B contract terms not reviewed — actual contractual obligations may vary",
      "Whether 'delivery records' as defined in the system include or exclude specific sub-documents (e.g., are PoDs stored separately?)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records include proof of delivery, timestamps, and commercial transaction data — not just routing metadata",
      "The platform processes transactions subject to VAT in EU/UK jurisdictions",
      "B2B customer contracts include data availability commitments or SLAs",
      "No separate archival system exists that would preserve legally required data outside the proposed deletion scope"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but auto-deleting delivery records at 90 days directly violates legal retention mandates (UK: 6 years, Germany: 10 years, France: 10 years), breaches B2B contractual obligations, and eliminates the company's ability to defend against claims within the statute of limitations. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the company's legal compliance posture.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "B2B customers", "Finance/Tax", "Executive leadership", "B2C consumers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day deletion entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — move records to cold/archive storage after 90 days (reduces cost) but retain for legally required periods",
        "C: Implement data lifecycle management — hot storage for 90 days, warm for 2 years, cold archive for statutory period, with automated legal hold capabilities"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. The legitimate business goal (reduce storage costs) can be achieved through tiered storage and data lifecycle management WITHOUT deleting legally required records. This gives engineering the cost reduction they want while preserving legal compliance. Option B is the minimum viable alternative. Option A maintains status quo if engineering cannot implement tiering."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days violates statutory record-keeping requirements in every EU/UK jurisdiction the platform operates in", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Delivery records deleted within the statute of limitations period make the company unable to defend against contractual and consumer claims", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "B2B customers who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting would lose access, constituting breach of contract", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Change was proposed without Legal, Compliance, or DPO review — framing it as 'infrastructure optimization' bypasses required governance", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" }
  ]
}
